content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcal PnecrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
Apil 7,2005
Jennifer Hosterman
2922 Chardonnay Drive
Pleasanton,CA 94566
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-05-044
Dear Ms. Hosterman:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTIONS
1. May you use campaign funds for a trip to New Yoili to speak before the
United Nations Delegation on the subject of the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty?
2. May you run this money through your current campaign account, or must you
open a separate account?
CONCLUSIONS
1. and 2. The contributions you collect may be used to fund the trip to New York
to speak before the United Nations Delegation on the subject of the Nuclear Non-
Proliferation Treaty if the purpose of the trip is directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose. The funds must first be deposited into your existing campaign
bank account.
' Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear atTitle 2,
sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
Lsl3g4l


.,}i*.q. 
-.rr.
File No. A-05-044
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are the Mayor of the City of Pleasanton. You are also a member of an
otganization called Mayors for Peace. In that capacity, you have been asked to speak
before the United Nations Delegation on the subject of the Nuclear Non-proliferation
Treaty during the first week of May in New York. You plan to leave the day before the
event and return home the day after the event. You will incur travel expenses, e.g., air
fare, lodging and meals. You intend to request contributions from the public to fund this
trip. In a phone call to the Fair Political Practices Commission on March 28,2005, you
indicated it was your intention to deposit the contributions in your campaign bank
account.
ANALYSIS
Pursuant to section 85201, all contributions received and all expenditures made by
a candidate must be made through a single campaign account. (Section 85201). (See
section 82015 and regulation 18215 for the definition of "contribution" and section 82025
and regulation 18225 for the definition of "expenditure.") All contributions received and
expenditures made must also be reported under the requirements of Chapter 4 of the Act.
(Sections 84200 et seq.)
Once an elected officer raises funds, there are rules governing the use of those
funds. These rules are referred to as the "personal use" laws. (Sections 895L0-59522.)
The general rule of the "personal use" provisions of the Act is that any expenditure of
campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably related to a political, iegislative, or
governmental purpose. However, where an expenditure confers a substantiil personal
benefit on the candidate or elected officer, the expenditure must be directly reiated to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Sectio n 89 5 12.)
Moreover, the personal use law sets forth specific standards concerning certain
types of expenditures. Section 89513(a)(1), which deals with travel, provides:
"(a) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay or reimburse
the candidate, the elected officer, or any individual or individuals
with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held
by a committee, or employees or staff of the committee or the
elected officer's govemmental agency for travel expenses and
necessary accommodations except when these expenditures are
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmentar purpose.
(1) For the purposes of this section, payments or
reimbursements for travel and necessary accommodations shall be
considered as directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose if the pairments would meet standards similar
to the standards of the Intemal Revenue Service pursuant to
rGiiiirq.


File No. A-05-044
Page No. 3
Sections 162 and.274 of the Internal Revenue code for deductions
of travel expenses under the federal income tax law.,,
Thus, the test set forth in Section 89513(a)(1) is whether there is a direct
relationship between a political, legislative, or govefllmental purpose and the trip you
wish to take. The travel and accommodations related to your attendance at the
conference meet these standards because they are directly related to a political,
legislative, or govemmental purpose. Therefore, you -uy r." campaign funds to travel
to the conference.
Internal Revenue Code Secti on t62(a)(2) sets forth general rules which permit the
deduction of all ordin ary and necessary traveling expenses *t it" away from home in the
pursuit of a trade or business. Pursuant to this section, campaign funis may be used for
"business portions" of the trip. To the extent that any day ii not a business day (such as
days on which there are no meetings or formal activities), campaign funds may not be
used.
Moreover, personal activities not related to a political, legislative, or
govemmental purpose may not be paid for with campaign funds, even if those activities
occur on a business day.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
f

Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

open a separate account?

Proliferation Treaty if the purpose of the trip is directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose. The funds must first be deposited into your existing campaign
